speaker: interviewer
text: Take nine of survivors of the Shoah. Interview with Itka Zygmuntowicz, March 3, 1996. So you were going to tell us about your cousin?

speaker: interviewee
text: The story started when I was in Ciechanow, Poland. And I lived-- in the same city lived my aunt, and her seven children, and her grandchildren, and in-laws. The whole family, she came. She was my mother's older sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ester Pazdziorek. And one of my cousins, my cousin Gitel, was also born and raised in Ciechanow, Poland, and was named after the same great grandmother Gitel than I. And I was eight years. It was in 1934. And she was born in April too. And she went to join her boyfriend in which later became the same year her husband. And I mentioned--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she join him?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jerusalem, Palestine. They got married. None of the relatives of his family-- he was also from Ciechanow, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did she go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She went in 1934. And when she left, we all cried, because we didn't know whether we'd ever see each other again. Unfortunately, Gitel is the sole survivor of her murdered family, I'm the sole survivor of my murdered family. Not one single person on my mother's side that lived in Poland at the time when Germany invaded Poland has survived. I'm the only one. I was fortunate enough that I survived. I was married. I realized that the only way to combat death is to create more life and joy in us and around us. Started to build a family. I was extremely thrilled when my first son was born the same year that the state of Israel in October. The state of Israel was established May 14, 1948. And my son was born October 3, 1948.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think about moving to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: You see, I wanted very much to go to Israel. But on my own, I couldn't. And when finally, when the [INAUDIBLE] offer to take the survivors, they offer to bring me here. So we left, and I had two children, two sons, in Sweden. When I came here--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the children were born?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America again?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came February 15, 1952. I settled Philadelphia and lived here ever since. In Philadelphia, I gave birth to two more sons. My first son is Erland. He was named after both my husband-- because my husband is the sole survivor of his family. So am I. So his named for his father and my father. So his Hebrew name is [NON-ENGLISH]. In Swedish, we lived in Sweden, called Erland. That was my other son, was born also in Sweden. Jerry. He was born November 28, 1951. And he was named after my husband's brother, Jakov. And then we came to America. And life is a whole series of choices. We both knew we wanted to have a larger family. Both came from a religious background. And after the Holocaust, it was extremely important for me. And I love children. I love people. I love life. And we had to decide what we want to do. And we decide that we want to have more children. God was good to us. And I gave birth to two more sons, Samuel. Since we didn't have a girl, so he was named after my mother and after my husband's mother. My husband's my mother was Laja. My mother's name was Simo. So he was named [NON-ENGLISH]. And English name, Samuel. I regret that I didn't call him Simha. Now, I wish that I could give any other name, but it's already too late. Because I realized I'm proud of who I am. Because I believe if you cannot be accepted for what you are, you sure will not be accepted for what you are not. So I wanted to have the names. And then my son, our youngest son, Michael, was born. I'm not going to go into the story. But both Michael and I were called a miracle, because I was extremely sick. I almost died when I gave birth to him. But somehow, we survived, and everybody was amazed. And Michael is a beautiful young man. And I made three nice bar mitzahs. The fourth bar mitzah, I said, I did for my friends. Now I wanted to go to Palestine. To Israel. If I lived from the gate of Auschwitz to go to Israel, 2,000 years, my people prayed next year in Jerusalem, and I was the lucky one, that I can go. So I want to go for all of them. Especially, I found out that after the war, everybody was looking for everybody. So on my father's side, his sisters survived because they were sent to USSR. And because of that, they survived. They only lost the youngest child, [NON-ENGLISH]. But not because he was killed. Because he died of starvation and sickness. But the rest survive, and they went to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you ever been to Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was when I went the first time I decided to go for my son's bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1978. In 1978, from 1934, I have not seen my cousin Gitel. So my cousin, by the time I came, the only cousin that survived on my father's side was [NON-ENGLISH], because her sister died in Israel, and my aunt died in Israel. They died the same day, because my cousin [NON-ENGLISH] had-- I forgot how you call this kind of pregnancy, that was outside the tubes. And she didn't understand. She didn't take care. So she died. And when they came to tell my aunt [NON-ENGLISH], she was so heart broken. So there are two graves together. They died same day, or it was in a day, or something like this. But anyhow when I came, that was the first time that I saw my cousin Gitel. She found out I am there. She came. And when she met my late son, Michael, for the first time, she gave him for his bar mitzvah a tallis and a yamaka.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then she asked Michael, what would you like, something else? And he said, I would like a little chain with my name. So she gave him this chain that I'm wearing. It says Michael. And Michael was wearing it for many years on his neck. And then when he got older, he said, Mom, why don't you wear it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would like to have a herringbone chain, they call it. You know, like a bigger one. And I had one. So I took it off of my neck, and I gave it. And he had-- it didn't take long. I received the call from Israel that my cousin Gitel died. She still had a chance to see me. And I don't need to tell you  the reunion. And from her, I got some photographs. And then, unfortunately, it was 1990, in May. It was supposed to be the graduation of my son Jerry Jakov. Was supposed to graduate from engineering school in Cornell University. And Michael, at that time, was already graduated too, and had his own home, and did very well, both as far as liked with his friends, and had has his own home, $125,000 that he paid, a wonderful job, and was well-liked and respected. And he left on Thursday, and he said to me, Mom, Dad, please get ready, and I'm going to come and pick you up to take to the brother's graduation. Friday, the police came in the middle of the night. [SOBBING] And to tell me the most horrible news. My son Michael was killed. Michael is dead. My cousin Gitel is dead. I had to survive. And I saw, lived through, the unspeakable horrors of the Holocaust, rise and fall of Nazi Germany, of Hitler and the Third Reich, and lived to be in Israel and still to see my cousin Gitel. And this chain has a lot history of history. This is the most precious piece of jewelry for me. And when Michael died, he left a lot behind, a lot of love and friends. The only thing he could take with him is his tallis. And this is very precious to me because it has so much history. So I remembered what my grandmother said, 'With God, all things are possible.'

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he die?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a car accident. Instantly killed. I never said goodbye to my family, except for my cousin Gitel. And twice, once in 1934 and once before I left. Then in 1981, I went to the world gathering. And this is going to come the end of another story that we had done before. And they wanted me also to participate. They made also a little show, a documentary. Nothing like this, but it was a little bit. They looked for somebody. Everybody said, Itka, because I was speaking from the Holocaust since 1974. So I was invited to come. It was the last day of the march. And everybody was marching to the [INAUDIBLE]. Menachim Begin is the prime minister. And I cannot express the emotion. For 2,000 years, we prayed, and here the state of Israel from the gates of Auschwitz, the gate from hell to heaven. And I couldn't march, because they wanted me to be there in case the youth have questions and everything. And I had a badge. Everybody who came to the world gathering had a badge. And on the badge, just Itka Zygmuntowicz, USA. Everybody's country. We came with children, grandchildren, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: I went by myself that time. And when we came there, I was standing, and I asked somebody for a pen or a marker. And I said, who's going to know me? I couldn't expect family. But even somebody-- a friend, an acquaintance, a neighbor. So many years. I have a different name now. The first name is the same, but the last name is different. So I quickly wrote Frajman. I added to the Zygmuntowicz Frajman. I wrote Ciechanow. And I wrote Auschwitz. As I'm standing there, someone, [INAUDIBLE] from Landsdale, Pennsylvania, was bringing the American flag to present to Menachim Begin. And he was marching. And he looked at my badge. And he said, Ciechanow, huh. And I said, yes. The same time, yes, Ciechanow, huh. And then the man says to be, I'm from Ciechanow. I  said, what's your name? And he said Kalfus. I went berserk. I started to jump for joy, to cry for joy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't believe it. Kalfus is alive. And the people who were filming, said to me, [NON-ENGLISH], ask them for an address. He gave me his card, his business card. We went up there later. And when I came there, I found out-- and I realized later after I come down that he must be too young. That he is too young for being the father.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said, are you the father ? No, the father of Kalfus. And we went up there. And I found out that he's married and has a family, children, grandchildren. I don't remember for sure if he had grandchildren then or not. And he has a brother who survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I understood that you only have what you give away. That my mother's good deed and mine didn't go in vain. Three generations are alive, and God willing, many of them. I realized what it means to save one person's life, to save the whole world. And he invited me to his home. And the Ciechanow, a few who lived there who I didn't even know survived, made a banquet. And I was invited, and I was the guest of honor. And they asked me to say a few words. And before I started to speak, I said my Yiddish name and what my parish from home. After I finish speaking, an elderly woman comes over to me. And she started questioning me about who I am. And when she was sure, she said, she puts out her hand, and takes my hand to say, [NON-ENGLISH], you were my kindergarten student. Your father and mother brought you when you were five years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she said, I was wondering, how could she survive at that age? But I wouldn't ask that. And then she said, I left before Nazi Germany came in. And your family saw me off. We were friends. And your father gave me something. And he said, Malka, keep it. And she said, I'm going to send it to you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm so glad that you survived, and it's yours. I couldn't sleep a moment. But I wouldn't ask what it is, because I figured, if she wants to give me, she will. Then I didn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Malka Fuchs. I have a photograph from her. Because every banquet, they made a photograph. And I have a photograph with Kalfus. And what she gave me was the photograph we hired with my father when was seven years old. Again, I saw, you only have what you give away. But that still is not the end of the story. When we came here, we didn't have any relatives for many years. I was so happy for anyone who had a family. I remember my late son Michael, when we lived in two houses. Many apartments, but this was the second house I lived in America.

speaker: interviewer
text: I remember when we moved into the first home, we saw an elderly lady, may she rest in peace, Rose Fitch. He said, Lady, I don't have a grandmother. Would you please be my grandmother? And she was a kind lady. She lived to 96. And she was his adopted grandmother. And we didn't have a family. And my children always ask me, how come we didn't have a family? And I have one conversation. And I never thought that I'm going to be testifying on the Holocaust at that time. And I saved it, and now it's a poem that everybody wants to hear. If you want, I'll share it. So the reason that it affected our whole lives, because you can never replace the irreplaceable loss of a life, of a home, of a family. I know it's someplace. This was my conversation with my late son, Michael. I need to find it. Is it about your grandmother?

words.text: life, of a home, of a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. This is my conversation with Michael. My grandmother have a poem too. OK. 34. I'm going to contribute one of those poems, this book of poems, to Yad Vashem probably. This poem was written February 8, 1966.

speaker: interviewer
text: 'Who wrote on your arm, Mom?' asked my curious-- that's the name of the poem-- asked curiously my little boy one day. I looked sadly at my innocent child, and thought very carefully what to say. The Nazis tattooed this number on my arm. I told my son short and plain. 'Why did they do it, Mom?' He asked Father. My God, I thought, how can I explain? You see, my child, there was a man, Adolf Hitler was his name. He was very cruel and hateful, and inflicted on people suffering and pain. Oh, said he, and ran out to play. And I sighed with great relief. I wanted to protect him for as long as I could from knowing my deep sorrow and grief. He grew, and I could not protect him forever. So I answered his questions one at a time. He wanted to know, why we Jews are hated. Did we commit some kind of a crime?

speaker: interviewee
text: Excuse me. No, my child. I told my son, we have not committed any kind of crime. Yet we Jews have been victimized and slaughtered by pharaohs, [INAUDIBLE], and Hitlers all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you hate the Nazis for what they did to us? Now I have no grandparents, no relatives, like my friends. The Nazis murdered all my loved ones. They have blood on their hands. I know, my child, that you are hurting. But hatred and revenge can never restore the lives of those who perished in the Holocaust and the precious things we once had before.

speaker: interviewee
text: You and I who know what pain is must never inflict on others hurt and pain. We must try to build a better world, so the dead of our 6 million shall not be in vain. This was my message to my child. When I found Kalfus, I realized what it means to have something-- you can have something and give it away. And because of honoring of my grandmother, I wrote for her a poem. And I made it to a song. With your permission, I'd like to share it. Just one second. It's called 'My Grandma,' and it's written for my beloved grandmother, Chaye Sure Zebra, of blessed memory. I wrote this November 25, 1989. [SINGING] My grandma was old. My grandma was wise. She gave me so much love. She gave me good advice. My dear, sweet, old grandma so often used to say, my child, you only have what you give away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So don't spare a hug, a kiss, or a smile. Remember, dear child, we are here just for a while. So don't spare a kiss, a hug, a kiss, or a smile. Remember, dear child, we are here for just a while.

speaker: interviewee
text: I loved my sweet old grandma, but suddenly one day, my dear, sweet old grandma forever went away. But her loving words of wisdom are with me till this day. My child, you only have what you give away. So don't spare a kiss, a hug, or a smile. Remember, dear child, we are here for just a while. So don't spare a hug, a kiss, or a smile. Remember, dear child, we're here for just a while. And you only have what you give away. And since 1974, I was giving away stories about the Holocaust. I spoke to many, many people already, both in Philadelphia and in other cities. And I have enough letters to wall my whole house.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the letters is not important. It's what people you know-- because I have learned from the Holocaust a very, very important lesson. And it's summed up in a small poem. May I? Who do you speak to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who do you speak to?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you're speaking.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I speak, I spoke in churches, in synagogues, in universities, to small groups, to large group, to private groups, to Jewish, to non-Jewish, to all different kinds. In fact, I didn't do it from the beginning, but I have two books where people wrote their comments, plus albums and albums of letters, which is only just a small fraction of which I put in, because otherwise, I would have no place to put.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I'm looking for the poem. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS] OK. 37. Because I realized, just like a doctor, when you're sick, first you have to diagnose how sick you are, then he can heal you. And if we don't realize what may contribute to the Holocaust, how can we prevent another one?

speaker: interviewee
text: This poem was written May 13, 1983. And it's called, 'I Don't Want To Be Like Cain or Abel.' I don't want to be killed like my brother Abel. I don't want to be a killer like my brother Cain. I want to be my brother's keeper, and not to cause anyone suffering and pain. I want to make this world a better place to live, so that not one of God's children should ever know my deep suffering and grief. And what I believe is that if we don't become our brother's keeper, by not making the choice to do so, we become our brother's killer. Because the Holocaust didn't just happen because of the Nazis. It's the silent bystanders. Because one act affects us all. We only have what we give away. In the final analysis, the world is only as good or as evil as each individual. And like the Talmud teaches us, to save one life is like to save the whole world. Mengele was only one man. And he's responsible for the death of 400,000 Jews. Raoul Wallenberg was also one man. Schindler was one man. And they saved so many people. Can you imagine how many more there would be if we wouldn't die.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it that started you to talk about the Holocaust in public?

speaker: interviewee
text: The number. Because people started asking me questions even long before anybody started talking about the Holocaust. Plus I always-- I'm not saying in terms of being a good writer or a bad writer. But always was able to express feelings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Events, everybody can talk about. Feelings, people are a little more afraid. And also, I felt that I survived. And those who didn't survive, who will bear witness for them? If my parents would be killed in the street, would I keep silent, or would I bear witness?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, 6 million died, Jewish, and almost 5 million non-Jewish people. And regardless of who a person is, a human life is precious and irreplaceable. The broader life, the dead, affects us all. Now, I wrote a little piece. I don't even have it with me. I know it from memory. It has a few lines. Four groups of people dramatically influenced my destiny. My beloved family, who knew me and loved me, and empowered me with positive energy, Hitler's Nazis, who didn't know me, but hated me merely for being born Jewish, and in one single day, murdered in Auschwitz my entire family. The silent bystanders who didn't know me, and didn't care about my deep suffering and grief. And the Swedish Red Cross, who didn't know me, but liberated me from Nazi tyranny and bitter slavery. I am one and the same person. Yet four different groups of people viewed me differently and treated me differently. I was loved who I am, and I was hated for what I am not. Because they didn't judge me on the merits of my character or of my deeds. So I learned not to view myself through the eyes of others, and never to become like those who destroyed me. To resist spiritually the same way I did when the Nazis tortured my mother  and I.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: What's that called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It has no name. I just was speaking. I wrote once-- this was the poem which was--
